title: Bridge Essay: Speaking-Induced Suppression of the Auditory Cortex in Humans and Its Relevance

body.text: This bridge paper connects the esoteric/philosophical concept of **Daimon** with mechanistic science from PubMed/MEDLINE.
**Bridge rationale:** Daimonic voice — voice-hearing continuum, inner speech misattribution, corollary discharge.

**Speaking-Induced Suppression of the Auditory Cortex in Humans and Its Relevance to Schizophrenia.**. Biol Psychiatry Cogn Neurosci Neuroimaging.. PMID: 31399393. DOI: 10.1016/j.bpsc.2019.05.011. Speaking-induced suppression (SIS) is the phenomenon that the sounds one generates by overt speech elicit asmaller neurophysiological response in the auditory cortex than comparable sounds that are externally generated. SIS is aspecific example of the more general phenomenon of self-suppression. SIS has been well established in nonhuman animals and is believed to involve the action of corollary discharges. This review summarizes, first, the evidence for SIS in heathy human participants, where
